Action Load Score.definition: A score showing how much urgent follow-up work a salesman has.

Actual Revenue.definition: Revenue from HubSpot deal amount, if populated.

Annualized Revenue.definition: The estimated tuition value spread across the normal length of the program. This helps compare short and long programs fairly.

Average Days To Close.definition: The average number of days from lead or deal creation to close for records with close dates.

Average Days To Enroll.definition: The average number of days it takes for a lead or deal to become enrolled or closed won.

Average Estimated Revenue Per Enrolled S.definition: Estimated enrolled tuition divided by the number of enrolled students for the row.

Average Estimated Revenue Per Lead.definition: Estimated tuition opportunity divided by the number of leads for the row.

Average Response Time.definition: The average time from lead creation to first known engagement or response.

Average Sales Touches Before Enrollment.definition: The average number of logged sales activities before a student enrolled.

Close Rate.definition: Won countable deals divided by leads.

Cohort.definition: A group of students/leads grouped by start term, intake, enrollment term, or created month if no official cohort field exists.

Cohort Source.definition: The field used to assign the cohort: official cohort first, then start term, enrollment date, or contact created month.

Cohort Start Date.definition: The parsed or inferred start date used to sort cohorts chronologically.

Cold Lead.definition: A lead with weak or old signals, but not dead.

Contacted Leads.definition: Leads with at least one known call, email, meeting, note, task, or summary activity signal.

Cost Per Lead.definition: Vendor spend divided by paid leads when spend data is available.

Cohort Top Value.definition: The most common vendor, program, source, or salesman for the cohort based on lead count.

Dead Lead.definition: A lead that is likely not worth normal follow-up because it is lost, disqualified, not interested, junk, duplicate, very old, or has no activity.

Data Quality Issue.definition: A count of records that are missing fields or associations needed for reliable dashboard reporting.

Deal Owner Fallback.definition: Deal Owner is used only when Contact Owner is missing.

Deals Created.definition: The number of unique HubSpot deals associated with the lead group.

Estimated Enrolled Revenue.definition: Estimated tuition value of students who enrolled or closed won.

Estimated ROI.definition: Estimated enrolled or actual revenue minus vendor spend, divided by vendor spend. It is only meaningful when spend is provided.

Field Mapping.definition: The HubSpot property currently mapped to a dashboard field.

Total Program Revenue.definition: The full estimated tuition value of the degree or certificate.

Enrolled Students.definition: Students or deals marked enrolled, closed won, active, accepted, or otherwise counted as won.

Hot Lead.definition: A lead that is likely to need immediate attention because it has a high score from recent creation, paid source, open deal, recent activity, or qualified status.

Hot Lead Potential Revenue.definition: Open pipeline potential revenue attached to leads classified as Hot.

Lead Age Days.definition: The number of days since the lead was created.

Lead Status Summary.definition: A meaningful status assembled from enrollment, final lead, lead, lifecycle, deal stage, and lead temperature fields.

Last Refresh.definition: The most recent dashboard data sync status and timestamp.

Leakage Score.definition: A 0 to 100 score estimating how much paid lead value is leaking due to uncontacted or dead leads.

Monthly Program Revenue.definition: The estimated program revenue divided by the expected number of months in the program.

No Activity.definition: Leads with no recent logged activity or no known activity after the stated number of days.

No Next Activity.definition: Open leads that do not have a future follow-up or next activity date.

Old Open Lead.definition: Open pipeline cleanup items that need owner, status, activity, or close-out review.

Open Leads.definition: Leads or opportunities that are still open and not won, lost, or dead.

Open Pipeline Potential Revenue.definition: Estimated total tuition for open non-won, non-lost leads and deals.

Organic Leads.definition: Leads that are not classified as paid based on available source and campaign fields.

Paid Lead.definition: A lead whose populated HubSpot source fields match configured paid-source terms.

Paid Lead Leakage.definition: Paid leads that are being wasted because they have no owner, no activity, no next activity, stale follow-up, or no progress.

Paid Lead Potential Revenue.definition: Open pipeline potential revenue attached to paid leads.

Potential Revenue.definition: Estimated tuition value of open leads or opportunities based on the student's program or degree level.

Program Duration.definition: The normal time expected to complete the program, used to annualize and phase tuition revenue.

Revenue Confidence.definition: High when a program or degree is known, Medium when inferred from program text, and Low when unknown.

Revenue Per Lead.definition: Total estimated program revenue divided by lead count.

Revenue Realization Note.definition: A note explaining how total tuition is spread across the program duration.

Revenue Timing.definition: A way to avoid treating a 4-year bachelor's program the same as a 6-month certificate when looking at near-term sales performance.

Reviveable Dead Lead.definition: A dead or inactive lead that may still be worth one more attempt because it came from paid marketing, has a program interest, has contact information, or showed prior engagement.

Sales Efficiency.definition: How much revenue or potential revenue is generated per lead, per paid lead, or per sales touch.

6-Month Revenue.definition: The portion of estimated tuition expected over the next 6 months, capped at the total program value.

Stale Lead.definition: An open lead that has not had recent activity and may need follow-up or cleanup.

Total Leads.definition: The number of unique HubSpot contacts in the current filters.

12-Month Revenue.definition: The portion of estimated tuition expected over the next 12 months, capped at the total program value.

24-Month Revenue.definition: The portion of estimated tuition expected over the next 24 months, capped at the total program value.

Unclear Revenue Attribution.definition: Deals where revenue attribution is ambiguous because contact/deal ownership or associations are incomplete.

Uncontacted Leads.definition: Leads without a known call, email, meeting, note, task, or summary activity signal.

Vendor.definition: The paid lead provider or marketing source, such as Atra, Google, Meta, or KlientBoost.

Vendor Confidence.definition: How confidently the dashboard identified the vendor and which HubSpot field supplied the vendor signal.

Warm Lead.definition: A lead that has some positive signals but is not urgent yet.

Archive / Remove Leads.action: Confirm the reason, then remove, archive, or suppress from normal sales follow-up.
Archive / Remove Leads.what: Records that appear invalid, duplicate, disqualified, or no longer useful for normal outreach.
Archive / Remove Leads.why: Keeping these in active lists makes performance look worse and distracts sales teams.

Calculated Metrics.action: Use the recommendation to decide whether to call, email, schedule, or close out the lead.
Calculated Metrics.what: Single-student timing, touch, and temperature metrics.
Calculated Metrics.why: They show how quickly and deeply the student has been worked.

Cleanup Needed.action: Assign owners, set next activities, fix missing statuses, and close out stale records.
Cleanup Needed.what: Records with missing status, invalid status, old open age, inactive owner, or no next activity.
Cleanup Needed.why: Pipeline hygiene keeps reports accurate and makes follow-up lists trustworthy.

Cohort Analysis.action: Compare cohorts for revenue, conversion, and degree mix; investigate unusual drops or spikes.
Cohort Analysis.what: Lead and revenue performance grouped by cohort or created month when no official cohort exists.
Cohort Analysis.why: Cohorts show whether recent intakes are improving or weakening over time.

Cohort Characteristics.action: Use the mix tables to understand which channels, programs, owners, and lead temperatures shaped the selected cohort.
Cohort Characteristics.what: A selected cohort's source, vendor, campaign, program, degree, salesman, temperature, and paid/organic mix.
Cohort Characteristics.why: The same enrollment rate can come from very different lead quality and channel mixes.

Cohort Comparison.action: Compare cohorts side by side and investigate cohorts with low enrollment, high leakage, high dead rate, or slow enrollment.
Cohort Comparison.what: Enrollment rate, revenue per lead, days to enroll, touches to enroll, paid leakage, and dead rate by cohort.
Cohort Comparison.why: Side-by-side comparison shows whether newer cohorts are getting better or worse.

Cohort Summary Table.action: Sort by enrollment rate, revenue, response time, or top source to identify strong and weak cohorts.
Cohort Summary Table.what: One row per cohort with size, paid share, enrollment, revenue, speed, touches, top vendor, top program, and top salesman.
Cohort Summary Table.why: It summarizes both cohort outcomes and the effort required to produce those outcomes.

Cohort by Degree Level.action: Use annualized and 12-month revenue when comparing short and long programs.
Cohort by Degree Level.what: Cohort performance split by degree level.
Cohort by Degree Level.why: Degree mix can explain changes in total tuition value.

Data Quality and Field Mapping.action: Fix missing owners, missing sources, weak field mappings, and attribution gaps before using KPIs for decisions.
Data Quality and Field Mapping.what: The health of the HubSpot fields feeding the dashboard.
Data Quality and Field Mapping.why: Bad or missing source fields can make revenue, vendor, and owner performance misleading.

Daily Action Center.action: Work the highest-priority rows first, then log activity, set next activity, or close out bad records.
Daily Action Center.what: A daily operating queue of hot leads, paid lead leakage, stale open deals, and reviveable dead leads.
Daily Action Center.why: It turns the dashboard into a practical follow-up list instead of only a reporting view.

Definitions.action: Use this page as the glossary when reviewing scorecards or exported tables.
Definitions.what: Plain-English definitions for calculated metrics, statuses, and revenue fields.
Definitions.why: Shared definitions prevent dashboard users from interpreting the same number differently.

Deal Owner Fallback Records.action: Fill Contact Owner where possible so salesman performance remains consistent.
Deal Owner Fallback Records.what: Records where Deal Owner was used because Contact Owner was missing.
Deal Owner Fallback Records.why: Contact Owner is the primary salesman field, so fallback records need review.

Degree Revenue.action: Use annualized and 12-month revenue when comparing short certificates with longer degree programs.
Degree Revenue.what: Revenue grouped by degree level and program using total, 6/12/24-month, and annualized tuition estimates.
Degree Revenue.why: Total tuition alone can overstate longer programs when comparing near-term sales performance.

Enrollment Path.action: Compare each milestone count to find where leads stop moving, then inspect the stuck status or owner.
Enrollment Path.what: A simple path from lead created to contacted, application or deal, enrolled or closed won, and closed lost or dead.
Enrollment Path.why: It shows progression without assuming a perfect ordered HubSpot funnel.

Executive Overview.action: Use this page to spot the biggest trend or risk, then drill into owner, vendor, pipeline, or cohort pages.
Executive Overview.what: The top-level health of leads, revenue, conversion, and source mix.
Executive Overview.why: It gives a fast read on whether sales performance is improving and where to investigate.

Fields Being Used.action: Review the mapped HubSpot fields and refresh data after any HubSpot property changes.
Fields Being Used.what: The HubSpot properties currently mapped into dashboard fields.
Fields Being Used.why: It explains exactly which source fields power each metric.

Fields Removed.action: Check removed fields when expected data is missing from a chart.
Fields Removed.what: Fields ignored because they were missing, empty, or not reliable enough.
Fields Removed.why: This prevents charts from using sparse or misleading data.

Hot vs Dead Leads.action: Work hot leads first, review reviveable dead leads, and archive records that should not be active.
Hot vs Dead Leads.what: Lead temperature distribution and inactivity risk.
Hot vs Dead Leads.why: It separates urgent follow-up from cleanup work.

Journey Events.action: Look for gaps between creation, first contact, deal creation, enrollment, and last activity.
Journey Events.what: A timeline of contact, activity, deal, application, and enrollment events.
Journey Events.why: The sequence shows where a student's journey accelerated or stalled.

Journey Stage Revenue.action: Use stuck high-value stages as follow-up priorities.
Journey Stage Revenue.what: Revenue grouped by lifecycle and deal stage.
Journey Stage Revenue.why: It shows where tuition value sits in the student journey.

Lead Drilldown.action: Download this table when you need row-level follow-up lists or audit details.
Lead Drilldown.what: The contact/deal-level records behind the scorecards.
Lead Drilldown.why: It lets managers move from summary metrics to the exact leads behind them.

Lead Status Summary.action: Prioritize high-value statuses with many stale, hot, or paid leads.
Lead Status Summary.what: Lead counts, paid mix, temperature, enrollment, and revenue by meaningful status.
Lead Status Summary.why: It answers where the pipeline stands right now.

Low Population Fields.action: Avoid making decisions from fields with very low fill rates unless you first improve data capture.
Low Population Fields.what: Mapped or discovered fields with sparse data.
Low Population Fields.why: Sparse fields can make charts incomplete or biased.

Missing Fields.action: Add, map, or populate these HubSpot fields if the related dashboard feature matters.
Missing Fields.what: Expected fields that are missing or unusable.
Missing Fields.why: Missing fields explain hidden charts and fallback behavior.

Paid Leads Performance.action: Investigate vendors or campaigns with high spend, low contact rates, leakage, or weak revenue per lead.
Paid Leads Performance.what: Performance of paid marketing leads, campaigns, vendors, revenue, and leakage.
Paid Leads Performance.why: Paid leads cost money, so weak follow-up or low conversion has direct budget impact.

Paid Leads With No Activity.action: Assign ownership and schedule immediate follow-up or suppress the record.
Paid Leads With No Activity.what: Paid leads with stale or missing activity.
Paid Leads With No Activity.why: These are paid opportunities at risk of being wasted.

Pipeline Health.action: Use the bottleneck and cleanup tables to decide what to work, update, or close today.
Pipeline Health.what: A clear operating view of statuses, stuck leads, enrollment movement, and cleanup needs.
Pipeline Health.why: It replaces ambiguous funnel charts with questions the sales team can act on.

Revenue by Degree Level.action: Compare total, annualized, and 12-month revenue before ranking programs.
Revenue by Degree Level.what: Estimated tuition and realized revenue grouped by degree level.
Revenue by Degree Level.why: Longer programs naturally have larger total tuition, so time-phased revenue is needed for fair comparison.

Reviveable Dead Leads.action: Try one more targeted follow-up for promising records, then archive if there is no response.
Reviveable Dead Leads.what: Dead or inactive leads that still have signs of value.
Reviveable Dead Leads.why: Some paid or program-specific leads may still be worth a final attempt.

Salesmen / Contact Owner Performance.action: Use action load and paid leakage for coaching; use annualized and 12-month revenue for fair revenue comparison.
Salesmen / Contact Owner Performance.what: Contact Owner performance across action load, leads, revenue, leakage, and conversion.
Salesmen / Contact Owner Performance.why: It shows who needs help, who has valuable pipeline, and where paid leads are leaking.

Stage Bottlenecks.action: Review stages with many records stuck 14 or 30 days and assign a next step.
Stage Bottlenecks.what: Statuses where open leads have been inactive for 7, 14, or 30+ days.
Stage Bottlenecks.why: Bottlenecks show where leads are not moving forward.

Student Journey.action: Search a student before a call or review and use the timeline to choose the next action.
Student Journey.what: A searchable profile and timeline for one student.
Student Journey.why: It explains the full path from lead creation to activity, deal, enrollment, or loss.

Student Profile.action: Confirm owner, source, program, revenue, and status before interpreting the timeline.
Student Profile.what: The key identity, source, academic, status, and revenue fields for the selected student.
Student Profile.why: A clean profile prevents follow-up based on the wrong owner, program, or source.

Tuition Configuration.action: Edit config/degree_tuition.json when CalMU tuition or duration assumptions change.
Tuition Configuration.what: The editable tuition and duration assumptions used for estimated revenue.
Tuition Configuration.why: Revenue estimates should be traceable and updateable without scraping the website on every load.

Unclear Revenue Attribution.action: Review associations and owners before using these records in performance decisions.
Unclear Revenue Attribution.what: Deals whose revenue could not be attributed cleanly.
Unclear Revenue Attribution.why: Ambiguous attribution can distort owner or source rankings.

Vendor Performance.action: Search vendors such as Atra, compare leakage and revenue per lead, and add spend data for ROI.
Vendor Performance.what: Paid lead outcomes by normalized vendor, source group, and campaign.
Vendor Performance.why: It shows which vendors create enrolled students and which vendors create wasted follow-up.

What It Took To Enroll.action: Use these medians and common paths to identify the outreach pattern most associated with enrollment.
What It Took To Enroll.what: Median response time, touches, enrollment time, and the most common source, vendor, program, owner, and statuses for enrolled students.
What It Took To Enroll.why: It shows the practical activity pattern behind enrolled students, not just the final count.
